reasoning:
Verified in /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/2026/wp-plugins-research/plugins/src/page-links-to/classes/plugin.php. The complete attack path is confirmed:

1. Hook registration (line 158): 'wp_ajax_plt_dismiss_notice' maps to ajax_dismiss_notice() — only the wp_ajax_ variant is registered (not wp_ajax_nopriv_), so authentication is required, but no capability beyond being logged-in.

2. Handler (lines 942-946): ajax_dismiss_notice() contains no check_ajax_referer, no wp_verify_nonce, no current_user_can() call. The only gate is isset($_GET['plt_notice']) && is_numeric($_GET['plt_notice']), which is not a security guard — it merely avoids PHP warnings and any integer value passes.

3. Sink (lines 1023-1028): dismiss_notice($id) calls update_option(self::DISMISSED_NOTICES, $notices) where DISMISSED_NOTICES = 'page_links_dismissed_options' (line 31) — a site-wide WordPress option, not per-user meta.

4. Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber, customer, etc.) can send: POST /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=plt_dismiss_notice&plt_notice=4 with no nonce. This permanently appends the integer to the site-wide dismissed notices array, suppressing that admin notice for all users on the site.

5. No sanitizer or guard was missed. The is_numeric() and (int) cast control input type only — they do not restrict who can call the handler or enforce any access level.

6. CSRF is also present (no nonce), meaning an unauthenticated attacker can trigger this via a logged-in admin visiting a malicious page, but the broken_access_control framing is the primary class.

Severity is medium: impact is site-wide persistent state corruption (suppression of admin notices for all users), not code execution or data exfiltration. Reachability claim in finding is correct.
